questions.question: If an economy's Nominal GDP is \$1,500 and the Real GDP is \$1,200, what is the GDP deflator for that period?
questions.explanation: GDP Deflator = (Nominal GDP ÷ Real GDP) × 100 = (\$1,500 ÷ \$1,200) × 100 = 125. Prices are 25% higher than the base year.

questions.question: Calculate the Real GDP growth rate if Nominal GDP increased by 7% and the price level increased by 3%.
questions.explanation: Approximate Real GDP growth = Nominal GDP growth – Inflation rate = 7% – 3% = 4%.

questions.question: Which of the following is included in the government spending (G) component of GDP but excluded from transfer payments?
questions.explanation: Government spending (G) includes purchases of goods and services, such as paying public employees. Transfer payments (social security, unemployment benefits) are excluded because they don't reflect current production.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a situation where the GDP deflator would be exactly 100?
options: Nominal GDP is greater than Real GDP
The economy is experiencing high inflation
The current year is the base year
Real GDP has doubled since the base year
questions.explanation: The GDP Deflator = 100 in the base year when current prices equal base year prices, making Nominal GDP = Real GDP.

questions.question: Which type of unemployment is characterized by a worker who has the necessary skills but is currently in the process of searching for a better job match?
questions.explanation: Frictional unemployment occurs when workers with marketable skills voluntarily search for better job matches. It is short-term and part of a healthy, dynamic labor market.

questions.question: How does the implementation of widespread artificial intelligence in manufacturing specifically impact structural unemployment?
options: It reduces structural unemployment by creating new types of jobs
It increases structural unemployment due to skill obsolescence
It only affects the level of cyclical unemployment
It has no measurable impact on structural unemployment
questions.explanation: AI displaces workers whose skills no longer match the requirements of the changing industry. This mismatch between available skills and available jobs increases structural unemployment.

questions.question: In a population of 20,000 people, where 12,000 are employed and 1,000 are unemployed and looking for work, what is the labor force participation rate?
questions.explanation: Labor force = 12,000 + 1,000 = 13,000. Assuming the 20,000 is the working-age population, LFPR = (13,000 ÷ 20,000) × 100 = 65%.

questions.question: Which of the following groups is excluded from both the numerator and the denominator when calculating the official unemployment rate?
options: New entrants to the labor force
questions.explanation: Discouraged workers have stopped looking for work and are not counted in the labor force. They are excluded from both the numerator (unemployed) and denominator (labor force) of the unemployment rate.

questions.question: Which of the following determines the weights assigned to different goods and services in the Consumer Price Index (CPI) basket?
options: Household expenditure surveys capturing spending patterns
The quantity of goods produced domestically
questions.explanation: CPI weights are derived from household expenditure surveys that measure the proportion of income consumers spend on each category of goods and services in the base period.

questions.question: Which of the following changes in the foreign exchange market would cause a rightward shift in the domestic Aggregate Demand (AD) curve?
options: An increase in domestic interest rates
questions.explanation: Currency depreciation makes exports cheaper for foreigners and imports more expensive for domestic consumers. Net exports (NX) increase, shifting AD rightward.

questions.question: Which of the following shifts the Short-Run Aggregate Supply (SRAS) curve but does not shift the Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS) curve?
options: A temporary increase in the labor force
A temporary change in nominal input prices
A temporary increase in the capital stock
questions.explanation: Temporary changes in nominal input prices (e.g., energy price spikes) shift SRAS by changing production costs but do not affect the economy's long-run productive capacity (LRAS).

questions.question: A firm paid its workers $42 an hour in 2019 when the CPI was 256. What was the real wage rate in 2019 to the nearest cent?
questions.explanation: Real wage = (Nominal wage ÷ CPI) × 100 = ($42 ÷ 256) × 100 = $16.41 per hour in base-year dollars.

questions.question: How does the economy return to long-run equilibrium following an increase in Aggregate Demand if nominal wages are flexible?
options: SRAS shifts right as wages fall due to higher output
SRAS shifts left as wages rise, restoring potential output
AD automatically shifts back left to its original position
LRAS shifts right to match the higher level of output
questions.explanation: With flexible wages, the inflationary gap (low unemployment) drives nominal wages up. Higher wages increase costs, shifting SRAS left until output returns to potential at a higher price level.

questions.question: Which of the following is true regarding the relationship between the actual unemployment rate and the natural rate of unemployment during an inflationary gap?
questions.explanation: In an inflationary gap, Actual GDP > Potential GDP. Labor demand is high, so the actual unemployment rate falls below the natural rate of unemployment.

questions.question: Calculate the size of the output gap as a percentage if Potential GDP is \$500 billion and actual Real GDP is \$480 billion.
questions.explanation: Output gap = ((Actual GDP – Potential GDP) ÷ Potential GDP) × 100 = ((\$480B – \$500B) ÷ \$500B) × 100 = (-\$20B ÷ \$500B) × 100 = -4%. The negative sign indicates a recessionary gap.

questions.question: Which of the following institutional factors is most commonly cited as a reason for downward nominal wage stickiness?
options: Long-term labor contracts locking in wages
The absence of minimum wage laws
questions.explanation: Long-term contracts prevent employers from quickly cutting wages during downturns. Minimum wage laws and worker morale concerns also contribute to downward nominal wage rigidity.

questions.question: Which phase of the business cycle is defined by two or more consecutive quarters of declining Real GDP?
questions.explanation: A recession is commonly defined as two consecutive quarters of negative Real GDP growth. It follows the Peak and ends at the Trough.

questions.question: If an increase in disposable income of \$5,000 leads to an increase in savings of \$1,000, what is the Marginal Propensity to Save (MPS)?
questions.explanation: MPS = ΔSaving ÷ ΔDisposable Income = \$1,000 ÷ \$5,000 = 0.2. So 20% of additional income is saved.

questions.question: What is the Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) if the slope of the savings function is 0.3?
questions.explanation: The slope of the savings function is MPS. MPC = 1 – MPS = 1 – 0.3 = 0.7. So 70% of additional income is consumed.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary determinant of a change in autonomous consumption that is independent of current income?
options: A change in current disposable income
A change in the marginal propensity to consume
questions.explanation: Autonomous consumption shifts due to changes in wealth, expectations, consumer confidence, or access to credit. These factors operate independently of current income levels.

questions.question: Given the consumption function C = 150 + 0.9Y, what is the level of Real GDP (Y) where aggregate saving is exactly zero?
questions.explanation: S = Y – C = Y – (150 + 0.9Y) = 0.1Y – 150. Set S = 0: 0.1Y = 150 → Y = \$1,500. At this income level, all income is consumed.

questions.question: What is the total change in Real GDP if the government increases spending by \$10 billion and the MPC is 0.75?
questions.explanation: Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 – MPC) = 1 ÷ 0.25 = 4. ΔGDP = 4 × \$10B = \$40 billion.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the effect of a \$50 billion tax cut on Aggregate Demand if the MPC is 0.8?
questions.explanation: Tax multiplier = –MPC ÷ (1 – MPC) = –0.8 ÷ 0.2 = –4. Change in AD = –4 × (–\$50B) = +\$200 billion increase.

questions.question: Which of the following is a discretionary expansionary fiscal policy intended to close a recessionary gap?
options: Unemployment insurance payments increasing during a recession
A newly legislated infrastructure spending program
Progressive income taxes collecting more revenue during an expansion
Food stamp benefits automatically rising as incomes fall
questions.explanation: Discretionary fiscal policy requires new legislation. A new infrastructure program is a deliberate policy action, unlike automatic stabilizers (unemployment insurance, progressive taxes, food stamps).

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal policy actions would decrease the government's budget deficit while simultaneously slowing Aggregate Demand?
options: Increasing government spending and cutting taxes
Decreasing government spending and raising taxes
Increasing government spending and raising taxes
Decreasing taxes and keeping spending constant
questions.explanation: Cutting spending reduces outlays and raising taxes increases revenue—both reduce the deficit. Both actions also reduce AD, slowing the economy (contractionary fiscal policy).

questions.question: Which central bank action is intended to decrease the total volume of lending in the banking system?
options: Lowering the target overnight interest rate
Purchasing government bonds on the open market
Raising the target overnight interest rate
Reducing the collateral requirements for loans
questions.explanation: Raising the target overnight rate increases banks' cost of funds, which is passed on to borrowers through higher lending rates. This reduces borrowing and the volume of lending throughout the banking system.

questions.question: Which action by the central bank would result in a decrease in the price of government bonds and an increase in interest rates?
options: Open market purchase of government bonds
Open market sale of government bonds
questions.explanation: Selling bonds increases supply in the bond market, driving bond prices down. Lower bond prices correspond to higher yields (interest rates). This is contractionary monetary policy.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an automatic stabilizer that functions during a period of rising unemployment?
options: The central bank lowering interest rates
questions.explanation: Unemployment insurance is an automatic stabilizer—it increases payouts when unemployment rises without requiring new legislation, supporting disposable income and cushioning the downturn.

questions.question: According to the balanced budget multiplier, what is the net effect on Real GDP if the government increases both spending and taxes by \$15 billion?
questions.explanation: The balanced budget multiplier equals 1. When G and T increase equally by \$15B, Real GDP increases by exactly \$15 billion.

questions.question: Which of the following occurs to the national debt when the government runs a budget surplus?
options: The national debt equals the budget surplus
questions.explanation: A budget surplus (revenues > spending) allows the government to pay down existing debt. The national debt decreases as past borrowing is repaid.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the role of the discount rate (or bank rate) in a central bank's corridor system?
options: It sets the floor for the overnight market rate
It determines the exchange rate target
It is the rate paid on government bonds
questions.explanation: In a corridor system, the central bank's lending rate (discount/bank rate) forms the ceiling. Commercial banks will not pay more than this rate to borrow overnight, as they can access funds directly from the central bank at this rate.

questions.question: How does an increase in the target overnight rate affect the quantity of money demanded by the public?
options: Increases the quantity of money demanded
Decreases the quantity of money demanded
Has no effect on money demand
Shifts the money demand curve to the right
questions.explanation: Higher interest rates increase the opportunity cost of holding non-interest-bearing money. The quantity of money demanded decreases as people shift into interest-bearing assets.

questions.question: What is the impact of a decrease in the overnight rate on the net present value (NPV) of corporate investment projects?
options: NPV decreases because borrowing becomes cheaper
NPV increases because the discount rate falls
NPV is unaffected by interest rate changes
NPV increases only for government projects
questions.explanation: A lower discount rate (interest rate) increases the present value of future cash flows. More investment projects have a positive NPV, encouraging greater business investment.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the movement of 'hot money' following an increase in a country's target interest rate?
options: Short-term capital flows out of the country seeking lower rates
Short-term capital flows into the country seeking higher returns
No change in capital flows occurs
questions.explanation: Higher interest rates attract 'hot money'—short-term capital inflows from foreign investors seeking better returns. This increases demand for the domestic currency.

questions.question: Which of the following exchange rate outcomes is expected when a central bank initiates an expansionary monetary policy while foreign rates remain unchanged?
questions.explanation: Lower domestic rates reduce returns relative to foreign assets. Investors sell domestic currency to invest abroad, causing depreciation.

questions.question: Which component of Aggregate Demand is most sensitive to changes in the central bank's policy interest rate?
questions.explanation: Investment (business capital and residential construction) is the most interest-sensitive component of AD. Higher rates directly increase borrowing costs, reducing the number of profitable investment projects.

questions.question: A liquidity trap occurs when the policy interest rate is ________.
questions.explanation: A liquidity trap occurs when the policy rate is at or near zero, making conventional expansionary monetary policy ineffective. At near-zero rates, the opportunity cost of holding cash is negligible, and increases in the money supply are hoarded rather than spent or lent.

questions.question: In a four-sector circular flow model, which of the following is considered an injection into the domestic economy?
questions.explanation: In a four-sector model, injections are: Investment (I), Government spending (G), and Exports (X). Exports bring foreign spending into the domestic circular flow.

questions.question: Which combination of fiscal and monetary policy would be most appropriate for an economy experiencing high unemployment and low inflation?
options: Contractionary fiscal policy and contractionary monetary policy
Expansionary fiscal policy and expansionary monetary policy
Expansionary fiscal policy and contractionary monetary policy
Contractionary fiscal policy and expansionary monetary policy
questions.explanation: High unemployment with low inflation suggests a recessionary gap. Both expansionary fiscal policy (increased G, lower T) and expansionary monetary policy (lower rates) can stimulate AD without immediate inflationary risk.

questions.question: Which of the following would cause a leftward and downward shift of the Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC)?
options: A rightward shift in Aggregate Demand
questions.explanation: A leftward/downward SRPC shift means lower inflation at every unemployment rate. This results from positive supply shocks (e.g., cheaper inputs) or lower expected inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following describes the relationship between the unemployment rate and the inflation rate at the non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment (NAIRU)?
questions.explanation: NAIRU is the unemployment rate at which inflation is stable—neither accelerating nor decelerating. It corresponds to the natural rate of unemployment and the vertical LRPC.

questions.question: Tight monetary policy by the central bank typically leads to ________.
questions.explanation: Tight (contractionary) monetary policy raises interest rates, which reduces borrowing, investment, and consumption. Aggregate Demand shifts left, and in the short run, output falls and unemployment rises until wages and prices fully adjust.

questions.question: If the nominal wage remains constant while the price level falls, what is the resulting change in the real wage?
questions.explanation: Real wage = Nominal wage ÷ Price level. If nominal wage is constant and price level falls (deflation), the real wage rises—purchasing power increases.

questions.question: Which of the following represents the formula for calculating the percentage change in the real wage?
options: %Δ Real wage = %Δ Nominal wage + Inflation rate
%Δ Real wage = %Δ Nominal wage – Inflation rate
%Δ Real wage = Inflation rate – %Δ Nominal wage
%Δ Real wage = %Δ Nominal wage × Inflation rate
questions.explanation: Approximate % change in real wage = % change in nominal wage – Inflation rate. This adjusts nominal wage growth for the erosion of purchasing power.

questions.question: What is the state of the output gap when the actual rate of unemployment is equal to the natural rate of unemployment?
questions.explanation: When actual unemployment = natural rate, Real GDP = Potential GDP. The output gap is zero, and the economy is in long-run equilibrium at full employment.

questions.question: Which of the following identifies the output gap that occurs during a period of stagflation?
options: Positive output gap with low inflation
Recessionary output gap with high inflation
Zero output gap with high inflation
Positive output gap with high inflation
questions.explanation: Stagflation—stagnant output + inflation—is caused by negative supply shocks. Output falls below potential (recessionary gap) while prices rise due to higher costs.

questions.question: Which of the following would cause the real exchange rate of the domestic currency to depreciate, assuming the nominal exchange rate is constant?
options: Domestic inflation is higher than foreign inflation
Domestic inflation is lower than foreign inflation
Both countries have equal inflation rates
questions.explanation: Real exchange rate = Nominal rate × (Domestic P ÷ Foreign P). If domestic inflation is lower, the domestic price ratio falls, causing real depreciation—domestic goods become relatively cheaper.

questions.question: If the real exchange rate between two countries is equal to 1, what does this imply about the purchasing power parity (PPP) between them?
options: Goods cost different amounts in both countries
Goods cost the same in both countries
The nominal exchange rate must also be 1
questions.explanation: A real exchange rate of 1 means identical baskets of goods cost the same in both countries when measured in a common currency. This is the PPP condition—the law of one price for a basket of goods.

questions.question: Which of the following would cause the demand curve for the domestic currency to shift to the right in the foreign exchange market?
options: A fall in foreign demand for domestic exports
A rise in foreign investment into the domestic economy
Domestic residents buying more foreign imports
A fall in domestic interest rates relative to foreign rates
questions.explanation: Higher domestic interest rates attract foreign capital inflows. Foreign investors must buy domestic currency to invest, increasing demand and shifting the demand curve rightward.

questions.question: Which of the following results from a significant appreciation of the domestic currency regarding domestic Aggregate Demand?
options: AD shifts left because net exports decrease
AD shifts right because net exports increase
AD is unaffected by currency appreciation
AD shifts right because imports become more expensive
questions.explanation: Currency appreciation makes exports more expensive for foreigners (exports fall) and imports cheaper for domestic consumers (imports rise). Net exports (X – M) decrease, shifting AD left.
